VO_EN: There's a scar on your side.
Long. Curved. Like something was pulled out from under your ribs.
You pull your shirt down.
Not fast. Just enough so the fabric covers it.
You've done this so many times your hand doesn't think anymore.
Every morning before you leave the house.
Every time someone stands behind you in line.
The fabric catches on the raised edge sometimes.
A small pull. Like a thread snagging on something it shouldn't.
You don't flinch anymore.
Some people have birthmarks. Moles. Things they were born with.
You have this.
They said it grows back.
So you believed them.
The field hasn't grown anything in two seasons.
You walk out every morning anyway because that's what you do.
The soil cracks under your feet like something dry breaking apart.
You bend down and press your thumb into the dirt.
It turns to dust between your fingers.
Nothing holds.
Your child sits on the floor inside the house.
Not playing. Not crying. Just sitting there like that's a thing to do.
You don't know when children stop asking for food.
But yours stopped.
You count what's left in the jar.
Rice. Some salt. A small bag of dried something you can't name anymore.
Three days. Maybe four if you skip yours.
You've been skipping for a while now.
Your stomach doesn't even complain anymore.
You don't remember when that started.
The man comes on Tuesdays.
He doesn't knock.
He stands at the edge of your yard and waits.
Not close enough to seem aggressive.
Not far enough to ignore.
Just at the edge. Like he's part of the fence.
You know what he's waiting for.
Everyone on this road knows.
You don't have it.
You didn't have it last week. Or the week before.
He leaves when the sun moves past the tree at the corner.
Every Tuesday like a clock that doesn't need winding.
You watch him go from the doorway.
Your hands are still wet from washing rice.
You wipe them on your shirt. Twice.
He'll be back.
Your neighbor came back three weeks ago.
He was gone for five days.
Nobody asked where.
Around here people leave and come back and nobody counts the days.
But he came back with money.
Not a lot. But more than anyone here has seen in months.
He bought new sheets for his roof.
His wife got sandals. The kind with the plastic straps.
Someone asked him how.
He said a word you'd heard before but never paid attention to.
He touched his side when he said it.
Casually. Like adjusting his shirt.
And then he said the thing everyone keeps repeating.
It grows back.
He said it like he was talking about hair.
You don't think about it.
Not really.
But the number stays in your head.
You hear it again at the well the next day.
Someone's cousin did it. Went to the city for a week.
Came back fine. Walking. Talking. Working again like nothing happened.
The number is always the same no matter who tells it.
Five thousand.
You do the math against your debt while your child sleeps.
It doesn't cover all of it.
But it covers enough to make the man stop coming on Tuesdays.
Maybe.
You lie awake that night staring at the ceiling.
Your child breathes in the dark next to you.
Small breaths. Steady.
You touch your side under the blanket.
Just once. Just to feel it still there.
A paper is on your table one morning.
You didn't see who put it there.
A number is written in pencil on the back of a receipt.
No name. No address. No instructions.
Just the number.
The pencil is smudged where someone's thumb pressed.
You fold it.
Put it under the jar where you keep the salt.
Your fingers smell like pencil lead after.
You don't throw it away.
The woman two houses down is gone.
Her daughter says she went to visit family in the next province.
But her chair outside is empty.
Her goat is still tied to the post and nobody fed it.
The rope is starting to fray from the pulling.
Nobody asks where she really went.
You already know. Or you think you do.
That night you pull the paper from under the jar.
Your child coughs in the other room.
A dry cough that shakes something loose inside them.
You unfold the receipt. The number is still there.
That's it. That's the whole thing.
The bus leaves at night.
You sit by the window with a plastic bag on your lap.
Everything you brought fits inside it.
A change of clothes. Your ID card. Thirty in cash.
The seat is warm from whoever sat here before you.
You press your back into it and feel the vinyl crack a little.
You watch the village disappear behind the dust.
The lights from the last house blink once. Then nothing.
The road gets smoother after the first hour.
You touch your side through the shirt.
Just once. Just to know it's still there.
Your body is still whole.
You keep thinking that.
You think it the whole ride like if you stop it won't be true.
The room is small.
Four plastic chairs. Fluorescent light. No windows.
Three other people sit apart from each other.
Nobody talks.
Nobody looks at each other.
You all know why you're here but nobody says it.
One of them keeps touching his side.
Same spot. Over and over. Like checking if something's still there.
You look away.
There's a crack running through the floor tile.
You stare at that instead.
Someone calls a name. It isn't yours.
One of them stands up and walks through a door at the end.
The door closes.
You don't hear anything after that.
The man sitting next to you rubs his hands together.
You do the same without meaning to.
Your jaw tightens. You're not sure when that started.
Your name. Or a number. You're not sure which.
You stand up.
The light turns on.
It's white. Too white. Everything is white and it hums.
You're lying down now and you don't remember getting on the table.
Someone puts something cold on your arm. A strap. A band.
It smells sharp in here. Chemical.
Like cleaning fluid mixed with something metal.
People around you but you can't see their faces.
Just shapes. Masks. Gloved hands.
You ask the question.
The one you've been holding since the village.
The one your neighbor answered so easily while touching his side.
How long until it grows back.
No one answers.
Someone says count backward from ten.
You get to seven.
Or six. You don't remember which.
The light gets softer. Then it's gone.
You wake up.
The ceiling is yellow. Stained.
There's a fan above you that doesn't turn.
You're in a different room now.
Thin mattress. Thin blanket. A plastic bag of pills on a small table.
The air is warm and still like nobody opened a window.
Your side hurts.
Not sharp. A deep pull. Like something stitched too tight.
You press your hand flat against it.
Gauze. Tape. Warm to the touch.
Something is different inside you and you can't name it.
Lighter.
That's the closest you get.
You try to stand.
Your legs work but the balance is wrong.
Like someone shifted your center of gravity to one side.
You reach for the wall.
Your fingers press flat against the plaster. Cold.
You try to remember what before felt like.
The way your body balanced when everything was still inside.
You can't.
Not the feeling. Not the weight.
Not what whole used to mean.
Just gone.
There's an envelope on the table next to the pills.
Thin. Not sealed.
You open it. Count the bills.
Count again.
Then you stop.
It's not what they said. Not half. Not a third.
You pick up the phone they left. Dial the number.
Nothing.
You dial again.
Same nothing.
The number doesn't connect to anything anymore.
A door opens in the next room.
You hear footsteps. Someone being walked in.
A chair scrapes against the floor.
Then quiet.
You lie back down.
There's a water stain on the ceiling shaped like nothing.
You stare at it because there's nothing else to stare at.
You wonder if the person in the next room asked the same question.
You wonder if anyone answered them.
You come home.
The bus ride takes longer this time.
Or it feels longer. You're not sure anymore.
Every bump on the road sends something through your side.
You hold the plastic bag against it like a cushion.
It doesn't help much. But you keep holding it.
Your child runs to you when you step off the bus.
You pick them up. Your side pulls sharp.
Something tears a little inside. Or it feels that way.
You hold them anyway.
Not yet.
The money pays half the debt.
The man on Tuesday takes the bills and writes in his book.
He doesn't nod. Doesn't say thank you.
Just writes and closes the book.
He'll be back. You knew that before you got on the bus.
You go back to the field the next week.
You pick up what you used to carry with one hand.
Your hands shake.
Your back won't straighten all the way.
You set it down. Try again. Same thing.
You used to do this without thinking about it.
Before it was just a thing your body did.
Now it's something your body fights you on every time.
You sit down at the edge of the field.
For a moment everything blurs - the dirt, the tools, the sky above the field - and then you blink and it's just the field again.
You don't mention it.
You don't remember when this stopped being easy.
No.
You do remember.
You're standing in your yard one evening.
The air is warm and heavy and smells like rain that won't come.
Across the road someone pulls their shirt.
Just for a second. Adjusting. Or not.
You see the edge.
Same place on the body. Same curve.
They don't look at you.
You don't look at them.
You don't need to.
A vehicle stops near their house the next morning.
It's not from here. The plates are from a different province.
No one comes out. No one goes in.
It just waits.
Then it leaves.
You close the door.
You don't like thinking about any of this.
You pull your shirt down.
The scar is still there.
It didn't grow back.
Nothing did.
And somewhere, someone is always next.
